Button: [{'Button': 'An interactive element designed to trigger a specific action or transition within a user interface, typically characterized by a distinct visual emphasis such as color, shape, or text that clearly indicates its function. It is positioned as the final or primary call-to-action element within its context, such as at the bottom of a form or after a selection, and is intentionally designed to be directly activated by user input to advance the user experience. Crucially, it must be the only element in its context that serves as a direct, actionable trigger for a process, distinguishing it from informational elements, navigational links, or non-interactive UI components that may appear visually similar but do not initiate a direct user action. Furthermore, it must not be a selection control that merely toggles or selects from a predefined set of options, such as radio buttons or checkboxes, which are designed to present choices rather than initiate a primary action. Additionally, it must not be a control that merely expands or collapses a menu or list, as such elements serve to reveal or hide content rather than execute an action. Importantly, it must not be a dropdown menu in its expanded state, as the expanded state is a container for selectable options rather than a standalone actionable element. Moreover, it must not be a control that merely confirms or applies a pre-defined selection or range, such as an "Apply Range" button that acts as a final confirmation for a user-defined selection, as this is a secondary action that does not initiate a primary process or transition on its own. It must also not be a control that is visually or functionally subordinate to another element, such as a radio button or checkbox, which is designed to represent a state or selection rather than to initiate a primary action. Furthermore, it must not be a link or text that serves as a navigational or informational element, such as a "privacy policy" link, which directs the user to another page or section but does not execute an action within the current context. Additionally, it must not be a UI element that functions as a text input field, search bar, or address bar, as these are designed for data entry rather than initiating an action.'}]
Check box: [{'Check box': 'A small, square-shaped graphical user interface element designed to allow users to toggle a binary selection state. It is visually characterized by a distinct filled or unfilled state, where the filled state (typically indicated by a checkmark inside the square or a solid square) signifies selection, and the unfilled state indicates non-selection. The element functions as an independent toggle control, allowing users to select or deselect an option without any group constraints, distinguishing it from radio buttons which enforce single selection within a group. It is a self-contained control that does not include additional contextual UI components such as labels, counters, or expandable arrows that are part of a larger composite control. The element must be presented in isolation, without being embedded within a larger container or list item that provides its own selection state, and must not be a visual indicator for a selection made within a list or menu, such as a checkmark inside a dropdown option or a selection marker within a table row. Crucially, it must be a minimal, self-contained toggle element that can be switched between filled and unfilled states, and must not be a solid-colored block or filled rectangle that serves as a visual indicator for a state without being a toggleable control. Additionally, it must not be a circular or round element, as circular shapes are typically used for radio buttons or other selection controls that do not function as standalone toggle elements. Furthermore, it must not be a square or rectangular element that is part of a larger composite control, such as a button or an icon, where the selection state is indicated by a visual marker (like a colored dot or icon) that is not a toggleable element itself. The element must be visually distinct from other UI components, such as radio buttons or toggle switches, and must not be embedded within a larger container or list item that provides its own selection state, such as a list of options where the selection is indicated by a checkmark inside a dropdown option or a selection marker within a table row. Importantly, it must not be a square or rectangular element that is visually styled as a selection indicator within a list or table row, such as a filled square used to denote a selected item in a data grid, which is not a toggleable control but rather a state indicator for a pre-selected item. Furthermore, it must not be a circular or round element that is used as a selection indicator within a list or menu, such as a filled circle used to denote a selected item in a list of options, which is not a toggleable control but rather a state indicator for a pre-selected item.'}]
Checked Radio button: ["{'Checked Radio button': 'An interactive single-choice selection control in a user interface, visually represented by a circular element that is filled with a solid color or contains a checkmark to denote selection. It is always part of a mutually exclusive group where only one option can be selected at a time. The selected state is clearly distinguishable from the unselected state, which is typically an empty outline or hollow shape. Each control is directly associated with a descriptive text label positioned immediately next to it, and the selection state is visually consistent across all instances in the group, regardless of label length or wrapping. Crucially, it must be a standalone selection element without any additional contextual UI components like dropdown indicators or expandable arrows directly integrated into its visual representation, and must not be part of a list or menu that expands to reveal options upon interaction. Furthermore, it must not be embedded within a larger container that visually represents a selection range or date picker, as such containers are not standalone selection controls. The control must be rendered as a distinct, self-contained graphical element that is not visually confused with other UI components such as checkboxes, toggle switches, or form fields, and must not be presented as a simple square or rectangle without a clear circular or checkmark indicator of selection. Additionally, it must not be a UI element that is part of a system-level interface, such as a browser's scrollbar or window control, which are not interactive selection controls within a user interface form or component. It must also not be a date picker trigger or any other UI element that opens a calendar or date selection interface, as these are not standalone selection controls but rather interactive containers for date selection. The visual representation must be a circular shape with a filled interior or a checkmark, and the selected state must be clearly indicated by a filled circle or a checkmark within a circle, not by a colored square or any other non-circular shape. Furthermore, it must not be a checkmark embedded within a dropdown menu or list that expands to show multiple options, as this represents a selection within a container rather than a standalone radio button.'}"]
Checked box: [{'Checked box': 'A graphical user interface element that visually represents a selected option within a list, characterized by a square or rounded-square container that contains a distinct checkmark symbol. This checkmark is rendered in a contrasting color and is positioned inside the container, which is typically accompanied by a descriptive text label. The element is designed to allow users to select multiple, independent options from a list, differentiating it from single-selection controls like radio buttons and binary toggle switches. The defining visual feature is the checkmark symbol being the sole interactive element within a dedicated square or rounded-square boundary that forms the primary interactive area, and this boundary must be directly associated with a selectable option in a list or form, not merely an icon within a larger container or header element, nor should it be part of a dropdown menu or list of selectable items without a dedicated container. Furthermore, the checkmark must be visually integrated as part of the interactive control itself, not merely an overlay or decorative element on a separate, non-interactive background or container. Additionally, the checkmark must be contained within a container that is part of a list of discrete, selectable items, where each item is presented as a distinct row or entry, and the checkmark is the only visual indicator of selection for that specific item.'}]
Dropdown box: [{'Dropdown box': 'An interactive user interface component that functions as a compact, rectangular input field designed to trigger the display of a list of predefined options. It is visually characterized by the presence of a downward-pointing arrow icon, typically positioned at the top-right corner of the field, which serves as a clear indicator of its expandable nature. The field may contain placeholder text or a selected value, and its appearance may change to indicate an active or focused state. This component is functionally distinct from static text fields, which do not offer a list of options, and from buttons, which are used to execute actions rather than to select from a set of alternatives. Crucially, it must be a standalone, self-contained control that presents a menu of choices when activated, and not a contextual menu or a list of actions that appears as a result of a user interaction with another element. Furthermore, the menu must be presented in a manner that is visually integrated with the input field, such as appearing directly below it, and must be triggered by the same control that displays the arrow icon, not by a separate interaction with a different element like a checkbox or radio button. Additionally, the menu items must be selectable options presented in a list format, not checkboxes or other interactive controls that allow for multiple or complex selections within the same field. Importantly, the component must not be part of a larger, non-interactive selection interface such as a date picker or calendar, where the selection is made by clicking individual items within a grid or list rather than from a dropdown menu originating from a single input field. The component must also not be visually or functionally confused with radio buttons, which are discrete, mutually exclusive selection controls that do not expand to reveal a list of options. Furthermore, it must not be a vertical scrollbar or a similar non-interactive, non-selectable UI element that appears as a contextual or system-level control, even if it is positioned adjacent to or within a list of options. Additionally, it must not be a browser address bar or a system search field, which are not interactive selection controls but rather input fields for navigation or search queries.'}]
Dropdown expand: The expanded view of a dropdown box, showing all selectable options.
Icon: A small graphic symbol representing an action or object.
Radio button: A circular UI element used for making a single choice from multiple options.
Scroll bar: [{'Scroll bar': 'A user interface element consisting of a vertical or horizontal track with a movable thumb or handle, used to navigate through content that exceeds the visible area of a container. The thumb is typically a distinct, smaller element that can be dragged along the track to scroll to different positions within the content area. It is characterized by a narrow, elongated track and a small, discrete thumb that slides along it, without any additional contextual elements like menus or lists.'}]
Text box: [{'Text box': 'A rectangular input field designed for user data entry, typically featuring a clear boundary and often displaying placeholder text or pre-filled content. It is visually distinct from other interface elements by its function as a primary point of text input, and is usually accompanied by a keyboard or other input method activation indicator, indicating its readiness for user interaction. It contrasts with static content, selection controls, or action buttons, which serve different purposes within the interface. Crucially, it is not a form field that is part of a larger, non-input-oriented container or section, such as a form header or a non-editable label area. Furthermore, it is not a selection control that presents a list of options with visual indicators for choice, even if it contains text or an icon. It must be an editable field that accepts free-form text input, not a dropdown or list selector that presents predefined options for selection. Additionally, it must not be a container for a list of selectable items, even if it displays text or a number indicating a count or state, as such containers are not designed for direct text entry. It must also not display a list of options with a selection indicator (like a checkmark or arrow) that implies a choice from a predefined set, as this indicates a selection control rather than a free-form text input field. It must not be a non-editable display field that shows a single selected value from a list, even if it has a visual indicator like a checkmark or arrow, as this is a selection control, not a text input field. Furthermore, it must not be a label associated with a selection control, such as a radio button or checkbox, even if the label text is displayed in a multi-line format or is visually prominent, as its purpose is to describe the control rather than to accept user input. It must also not be a scroll bar component, which is a navigational control for moving through content, even if it contains text or numerical indicators, as its function is not text input.'}]
